spec-driven-development.meta.title: Spec-Driven Development - DevAI Day Workshop
spec-driven-development.meta.description: Learn Spec-Driven Development with GitHub Copilot — write specs first, then let AI plan, break down tasks, and implement using GitHub Spec Kit.
spec-driven-development.sidebar.title: Spec-Driven Development
spec-driven-development.progress.label: Progress
spec-driven-development.step1.title: What is Spec-Driven Development?
spec-driven-development.step1.p2: Spec-Driven Development (SDD) flips the usual AI coding workflow. Instead of prompting an agent to "just build it" and hoping the result matches your intent, you first capture what you want and why in a clear specification — then let Copilot turn that spec into a plan, tasks, and code.
spec-driven-development.step1.p3: Traditional prompting mixes intent and implementation together in a single throwaway message. Spec-driven development separates them:
spec-driven-development.step1.li1: Intent (the spec) — The durable description of the problem, the desired behavior, constraints, and acceptance criteria. It is reviewed, versioned, and shared.
spec-driven-development.step1.li2: Implementation (the code) — A consequence of the spec, generated by the agent and regenerated when the spec changes.
spec-driven-development.step1.p4: When the spec is the source of truth, the code becomes easier to regenerate, review, and reason about — and the AI has a stable target to aim at.
spec-driven-development.step1.h22: Why Specs Matter for AI Coding
spec-driven-development.step1.li3: Better results — Agents perform dramatically better with explicit requirements than with vague one-liners.
spec-driven-development.step1.li4: Shared understanding — A spec is reviewable by humans before a single line of code is written.
spec-driven-development.step1.li5: Reproducibility — The same spec can drive consistent regeneration across models and sessions.
spec-driven-development.step1.li6: Guardrails — Constraints and acceptance criteria keep the agent from wandering off scope.
spec-driven-development.step1.p5: Think of the spec as the contract and the agent as the contractor. You refine the contract until it is unambiguous; the contractor builds to it.
spec-driven-development.step1.li7: Use it for new features, multi-file changes, and anything you'll need to review, hand off, or revisit.
spec-driven-development.step1.li8: Skip it for trivial edits, quick experiments, or single-line fixes where the overhead isn't worth it.
spec-driven-development.step1.li10: Plan — Let Copilot propose a technical approach.
spec-driven-development.step1.li11: Break down — Decompose the plan into concrete, ordered tasks.
spec-driven-development.step1.li12: Implement — Execute the tasks, verifying against acceptance criteria.
spec-driven-development.step1.p6: Over the next steps you'll practice this loop first with plain Copilot, then with GitHub Spec Kit — a toolkit that formalizes each stage with slash commands.
spec-driven-development.step1.strong2: Try it (2 min): Intent vs. implementation
spec-driven-development.step1.p7: Take a feature you recently built and write a single sentence describing its intent (the what and why) with zero mention of how it was built. If you can't avoid naming a function, framework, or data structure, rewrite it. This is the muscle SDD trains.
spec-driven-development.step2.title: Writing Specs with Copilot
spec-driven-development.step2.p2: Before reaching for dedicated tooling, you can practice spec-driven development with nothing more than a markdown file and GitHub Copilot. The goal: a spec precise enough that Copilot can implement it without guessing.
spec-driven-development.step2.p3: A useful feature spec is structured and testable. A simple template:
spec-driven-development.step2.p4: Keep specs focused on behavior and outcomes. Leave the implementation details (frameworks, file layout, algorithms) to the planning step so the agent can choose the best fit for your codebase.
spec-driven-development.step2.h22: Use Plan Mode to Turn a Spec into a Plan
spec-driven-development.step2.p5: Copilot's Plan Mode reads your spec and the surrounding codebase, then proposes an implementation plan for you to review before any code is written. This is the bridge between intent and implementation.
spec-driven-development.step2.li1: Open your repository in VS Code with the spec file visible.
spec-driven-development.step2.li2: Start a Copilot Chat session in Plan Mode and reference the spec: Plan the implementation of #file:cart-discounts.md
spec-driven-development.step2.li3: Review the proposed steps, push back on anything that doesn't fit, and iterate until the plan matches your intent.
spec-driven-development.step2.p6: Specs describe a single feature; instructions files describe how all work in the repo should be done. Together they give the agent both the "what" and the "house rules".
spec-driven-development.step2.strong2: Try it (3 min): Sharpen one acceptance criterion
spec-driven-development.step2.p7: Look at the discount-code spec above. Add one more acceptance criterion that pins down an edge case it currently misses (for example: what happens when a code is applied to an empty cart?). Phrase it as a concrete, checkable assertion — a value in, a value out.
spec-driven-development.step2.p8: You can also scope guidance with path-specific .instructions.md files so different parts of the codebase follow different rules.
spec-driven-development.step2.h24: Exercise: Write and Plan a Spec
spec-driven-development.step2.li4: Create specs/feature.md using the template above for a small feature in a sample repo.
spec-driven-development.step2.li5: Add a .github/copilot-instructions.md with 3–4 conventions for the repo.
spec-driven-development.step2.li6: Ask Copilot in Plan Mode to plan the implementation from your spec.
spec-driven-development.step2.li7: Refine the spec until the generated plan is correct — notice how clearer specs produce better plans.
spec-driven-development.step3.title: Introducing GitHub Spec Kit
spec-driven-development.step3.p2: GitHub Spec Kit is an open-source toolkit that turns the spec-driven loop into a repeatable workflow. It adds a set of slash commands to your AI coding agent and a project structure that keeps specs, plans, and tasks organized.
spec-driven-development.step3.li1: A consistent structure — Dedicated locations for your project constitution, specs, plans, and task lists.
spec-driven-development.step3.li2: Slash commands — /speckit.constitution, /speckit.specify, /speckit.plan, /speckit.tasks, and /speckit.implement that drive each stage of the loop.
spec-driven-development.step3.li3: Agent-agnostic — Works with GitHub Copilot and 30+ other coding agents.
spec-driven-development.step3.li4: Python 3.11+ and uv installed
spec-driven-development.step3.li5: Git and a GitHub Copilot-enabled agent (e.g., VS Code or Copilot CLI)
spec-driven-development.step3.h22: Install the specify CLI & Initialize a Project
spec-driven-development.step3.p3: Install the specify CLI with uv, then scaffold a project and choose your agent with --integration. Replace vX.Y.Z with the latest release tag:
spec-driven-development.step3.p4: Spec Kit adds its /speckit.* commands and a .specify/ + specs/ structure. In IDE agents the commands appear as slash commands; in GitHub Copilot CLI you select the agent with /agents (or address it directly in a prompt).
spec-driven-development.step3.p5: The constitution captures the non-negotiable principles your project must always follow — quality bars, architectural rules, testing expectations. The agent consults it during planning and implementation.
spec-driven-development.step3.p6: Spec Kit writes these principles to a constitution file so every subsequent /speckit.plan and /speckit.implement stays aligned with them.
spec-driven-development.step3.strong2: Try it (5 min): Scaffold and inspect
spec-driven-development.step3.p7: Run specify init on a throwaway project and open the generated .specify/ and specs/ folders. Then run a /speckit.constitution command with two principles of your own and open the resulting constitution file. What did Spec Kit add that you didn't type?
spec-driven-development.step3.p8: A Spec Kit constitution plays a similar role to a copilot-instructions.md file — project-wide rules — but it is woven into Spec Kit's structured planning commands so the principles are enforced at each stage.
spec-driven-development.step4.title: The Spec Kit Workflow
spec-driven-development.step4.p2: With a constitution in place, Spec Kit drives the rest of the loop through its /speckit.* commands. Each one produces a reviewable artifact you can refine before moving on.
spec-driven-development.step4.p3: Start with the feature, focused on intent rather than the tech stack. Spec Kit generates a structured specification file.
spec-driven-development.step4.p4: Now provide your stack and constraints. Spec Kit produces a technical plan that respects both your spec and your constitution.
spec-driven-development.step4.p5: Decompose the plan into an ordered, actionable task list. This is where the work becomes concrete and trackable.
spec-driven-development.step4.p6: Review the generated tasks for correct ordering and dependencies. Reorder or split anything that's too large before implementing.
spec-driven-development.step4.p7: Have the agent work through the task list, building the feature against the spec and constitution.
spec-driven-development.step4.p8: If something is wrong, fix the earliest artifact that's wrong — update the spec, re-plan, regenerate tasks — rather than patching generated code by hand. Keeping the spec as the source of truth is what makes the workflow repeatable.
spec-driven-development.step4.p9: Spec Kit adds commands that act like "unit tests for your spec" — use them to catch gaps before you generate code:
spec-driven-development.step4.li2: /speckit.analyze — Cross-artifact consistency & coverage check (run after /speckit.tasks, before /speckit.implement).
spec-driven-development.step4.li3: /speckit.checklist — Generate a custom quality checklist validating requirement completeness, clarity, and consistency.
spec-driven-development.step4.strong2: Try it (5 min): One command in isolation
spec-driven-development.step4.p10: Before the full hands-on, practice a single stage on its own. In a project that already has a spec, run just /speckit.tasks and read the generated task list. Are the tasks ordered correctly? Is any single task too big to implement safely? Reorder or split one before moving on.
spec-driven-development.step4.h26: The Full Loop at a Glance
spec-driven-development.step5.title: Hands-on: Build a Feature Spec-First
spec-driven-development.step5.p2: Put the full loop together. You'll take a small feature from idea to working code entirely spec-first, using Spec Kit.
spec-driven-development.step5.p3: Build a command-line "habit tracker" that lets a user add daily habits, mark them complete for today, and view a weekly streak summary. No UI framework required — keep it simple.
spec-driven-development.step5.li1: Initialize a fresh project with specify init habit-tracker --integration copilot.
spec-driven-development.step5.li2: /speckit.constitution — Require unit tests for all logic, no external dependencies beyond the standard library, and clear help output.
spec-driven-development.step5.li3: /speckit.specify — Describe the habit tracker's behavior and acceptance criteria (add, complete, weekly streak). Focus on what, not how.
spec-driven-development.step5.li4: /speckit.plan — Choose a language (e.g., Python or Node), a storage approach (a local JSON file), and a simple command structure.
spec-driven-development.step5.li5: /speckit.tasks — Generate the task list and review the ordering.
spec-driven-development.step5.li6: /speckit.implement — Let the agent build it, then run the app and tests.
spec-driven-development.step5.li7: Adding a habit persists it across runs.
spec-driven-development.step5.li8: Marking a habit complete twice in one day is idempotent.
spec-driven-development.step5.li9: The weekly summary shows the correct current streak per habit.
spec-driven-development.step5.li10: All logic is covered by passing unit tests.
spec-driven-development.step5.p4: When a criterion fails, resist hand-editing the code. Update the spec or plan and regenerate. Notice how much faster iteration becomes when the spec — not the code — is your source of truth.
spec-driven-development.step5.li11: Add a new requirement to the spec (e.g., reminders) and watch how a single /speckit.plan → /speckit.tasks → /speckit.implement pass extends the app.
spec-driven-development.step5.li12: Combine SDD with the Agentic Workflows path to automate spec-driven changes in CI.
spec-driven-development.step5.li13: Bring Spec Kit into a real repository and trial it on your next feature.
spec-driven-development.step5.li14: GitHub Spec Kit repository
